localBiteReport: The most current reports (within the past week, as of July 24, 2026) from Choctawhatchee Bay show speckled trout as the standout inshore bite — guides out of Santa Rosa Beach are reporting solid numbers with several quality fish in the mix, and one angler recently boated a 40-plus-inch, 26-lb bull redfish after a lengthy fight. Redfish are actively cruising the bay and beaches, with recent charter trips landing upper-slot fish and limits of reds, including productive sessions up creek mouths and along West Bay. Flounder have also been showing up on bay trips alongside trout and reds, and a 43-lb black drum was reported from a flats trip as recently as June 30. Water temp is 85°F. Reports from late June onward suggest summer tide conditions have improved, and while wind has occasionally required anglers to be selective about where they fish, the trout in particular are described as scattered but actively feeding throughout the bay.
localBiteSource: captainexperiences.com (Choctawhatchee Bay reports, July 2026), fishingbooker.com (Choctawhatchee Bay reports, July 2026) · Auto-refreshed via Claude API

aiSummary: Friday looks like a textbook summer morning on Choctawhatchee Bay — mostly sunny with a nearly calm NW wind and only a 1% storm chance before 1 PM, giving you a clean early window to work before the heat index climbs toward 101°F. The waxing gibbous moon is driving a strong tidal push, with high tide having peaked in the early morning hours and a falling tide running through the prime fishing window toward a 1:45 PM low — ideal for concentrating bait and predators on outgoing current. Recent reports confirm quality speckled trout, redfish, and flounder are actively feeding across the bay, with 85°F water temps making a first-light start the single biggest advantage you can give yourself today.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans a narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a current funnel that pushes bait through on both tides and concentrates trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum around the pilings year-round. The lee-side pilings are the prime ambush zone, especially on incoming tides. Being open water, this is the first spot to become uncomfortable or unfishable when wind picks up.
bridge.todaysCall: With NW winds at 0–10 mph today, chop should be minimal at the bridge, making it fishable through the morning. The falling tide running toward the 1:45 PM low is your best ally here — position on the lee-side pilings and work the current seam as bait gets funneled through the narrows. Slightly stained water and bright sun favor darker soft plastics or live shrimp tight to the structure; trout and reds will be tucked in the shadow lines by mid-morning. Get there at or just after first light (sunrise 5:56 AM) to catch the most active feeding window before the heat builds — the strong tidal push from this waxing gibbous moon should have current moving well. Wrap up before midday when exposed open water becomes a grind in the heat.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, plus trout and flounder at the mouth. The oyster bars at the creek mouth are the prime target, and tree cover keeps the upper bayou a few degrees cooler than the open bay while providing shelter from wind and weather. The upper creek transitions toward brackish and can hold bass alongside smaller reds.
alaqua.todaysCall: The falling tide toward the 1:45 PM low is money for the oyster bars at the creek mouth today — bait will be washing off the bars and reds will be stacked on the downstream edges. Hit the mouth structures at first light while the waxing gibbous moon's tidal push is still working and water temps are at their coolest. Slightly stained water calls for a noisy or high-contrast presentation — a gold spoon, chartreuse paddle tail, or popping cork with live shrimp are solid picks. The tree cover inside the bayou will help keep things comfortable as the heat index climbs toward 101°F, making this a spot you can fish later into the morning than most. Recent reports of strong redfish numbers across the bay, including a bull red over 26 lbs, are an encouraging sign for a spot with this kind of consistent year-round redfish population.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees lighter pressure than nearby Alaqua and LaGrange, meaning the fish here tend to be less educated and more willing to bite. The interior grass flats are classic sight-fishing territory for redfish in under two feet of water, and pompano occasionally show at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It is less protected than LaGrange when weather builds.
basin.todaysCall: Light and nearly calm NW winds today make Basin Bayou's interior flats very approachable — this is a good morning for sight-casting to reds on the shallow grass before the sun gets high and glaring. Work the falling tide carefully; as water drains off the interior flats toward the 1:45 PM low, reds will push toward the edges and the mouth where they're easier to locate. Slightly stained water means getting close and using noticeable presentations — a weedless gold spoon or a soft plastic on a light jig head works well in these conditions. Arrive at first light to capitalize on the most active feeding window and get your sight-fishing done before the bright midday sun makes the heat index oppressive. With less pressure than neighboring bayous, this is a worthwhile stop if the more popular spots feel crowded.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is considered the top trout producer among these locations, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside hold black drum and sheepshead, and the bayou offers the most protected water on this side of the bay, making it the reliable go-to when weather threatens. It consistently produces quality fish across multiple species.
lagrange.todaysCall: Today's falling tide toward the 1:45 PM low is perfectly suited for LaGrange's oyster bar mouth — set up on the downstream side of the bars as bait flushes off and trout and reds stack up to feed. Recent charter reports from Choctawhatchee Bay confirm quality trout in the 4–5 lb range are being found near structure, and LaGrange's legendary bars are exactly that kind of structure. Start at the mouth at first light, then transition to the interior grass flats for sight-casting reds as the tide falls and fish move into predictable ambush positions. Slightly stained water warrants a popping cork and live shrimp or a brighter soft plastic near the oyster edges. Nearly calm NW winds mean you'll have comfortable access to both the mouth and the protected interior — make the most of the early morning before the heat index tops 100°F.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay, where incoming tides push baitfish up the creek and concentrate redfish and trout in the bends and along grass edges. The creek mouth is a prime flounder zone, and the upper section transitions toward brackish water that can hold bass alongside smaller reds. Short and well-protected, it is the safest fishery on this list when weather threatens.
fourmile.todaysCall: Today's tide is falling through the prime morning window, which means bait is draining back out of the creek — position near the bends and grass edges in the lower section where reds and trout will be waiting to intercept it. The creek mouth is also worth a few drifts for flounder as the outgoing current sweeps through; recent bay-wide reports confirm flounder are showing on charter trips across Choctawhatchee Bay. Slightly stained water is par for the course in a tidal creek environment — a scented soft plastic or live finger mullet on a jig head will draw strikes even when visibility is limited. Being so close to Shipyard Marina, you can launch efficiently and be fishing right at sunrise (5:56 AM) when feeding activity peaks under the waxing gibbous moon's strong tidal influence. This spot's short, protected layout also means you won't be grinding a long run back in the afternoon heat.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is one of the most productive trout flats on Choctawhatchee Bay, a signature spot for Santa Rosa Beach guides with trout consistently stacking on the grass in two to four feet of water during summer mornings. Spanish mackerel show at the mouth on moving tides, and the bayou sees regular fishing pressure but produces reliably. Factor in the longer run time from the 331 bridge area.
hogtown.todaysCall: Summer mornings are the peak window for Hogtown Bayou trout, and today checks every box — nearly calm NW winds, a falling tide, and a strong waxing gibbous tidal push all converge to set up ideal conditions on the grass flats. Be there at or just after sunrise (5:56 AM); the waning outgoing tide will concentrate trout in the 2–4 foot zone as bait funnels off the flat, and 85°F water temp makes early morning the only comfortable feeding window for fish and angler alike. Bright sun and slightly stained water is a trade-off — go with a natural-colored topwater early when light is low, then switch to a light jig under a popping cork once the sun is up. The mouth is worth a stop for Spanish mackerel on the falling tide before it bottoms out near 1:45 PM. Plan your run time accordingly and aim to be off the water before the heat index peaks in the early afternoon.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits inside Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville, offering a protected, scenic, and significantly less-pressured alternative to the bayous closer to the 331 bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek makes it an excellent flounder fishery, and the glass-calm state park coves are well-suited for sight-fishing redfish. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, and the furthest-from-Shipyard-Marina location means a longer run should be factored into your planning.
rocky.todaysCall: The extra run time to Rocky Bayou is worth considering carefully today — leave early enough to be fishing the coves at first light (sunrise 5:56 AM) when the combination of falling tide, strong waxing gibbous moon, and cooler air temps gives you the best shot at active reds and flounder before the heat becomes a factor. The glass-calm state park coves are ideal for sight-casting this morning given the nearly nonexistent wind forecast, and slightly stained water means getting within short casting distance before presenting a fly or soft plastic. Work the flounder zone at the creek mouth as the outgoing tide pushes bait through, then shift to the coves for redfish on the falling water. Mangrove snapper near the dock pilings can fill in any slow periods — a small live shrimp on a light jig head is hard to beat. The low fishing pressure here means fish are less leader-shy, a real advantage on a bright, sunny day with clear skies.
